title: Needle-felted robin
subtitle: A dimensional robin built from a compacted merino base with surface colour detailing
excerpt: Needle-felt a compact egg-shaped body in grey-brown merino, then work the orange breast, black eye, and warm-tan back colours on the surface with a fine needle. A good first dimensional needle-felting project: the robin's compact oval body suits beginners, and the orange breast is forgiving enough that minor unevenness reads as natural feather texture.
sourceNotes: Dimensional needle-felted bird figures are a well-established form in contemporary craft; the robin is the most commonly made British bird shape. The method follows the observation-led technique tradition: compact base, needle-in colour details, fine-needle surface finish.

content.text: This robin is built in two stages: a compact oval body base in grey-brown merino, then the orange-red breast, warm-tan back, and black eye added as
with a fine needle. The finished bird is about 6 cm long and 4 cm tall. It can stand upright if the base is pressed flat during drying, or sit on a branch with legs of twisted wire added at the end.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.
attrs.title: Needle safety

items.name: Merino roving, warm tan (back and head top)
Merino roving, black (eye and beak base)
Foam felting mat, at least 5 cm thick

content.text: Take 15 g of grey-brown roving and fold it into a rough oval ball: fold the length in half twice, then roll between your palms. Place on the foam mat. Using the size 38

, stab the surface all over in a grid pattern, rotating the form every 2-3 minutes to work all sides evenly. Continue until the form is firm and compact: the needle should meet noticeable resistance when you drive it in. This takes 20-25 minutes for a 15 g form. Shape the oval as you work: press the tail end slightly flatter and the head end slightly rounder.

Take a 5 cm length of orange-red roving and pull it to a thin, even sheet. Lay it against the front of the robin body, covering the lower two-thirds of the chest. With the fine size 40 needle, stab around the edges of the orange patch first, working the orange fibres into the grey-brown base. Then fill in the centre of the patch with shallow stabs (about 3-4 mm deep). The orange should blend at the edges into the base colour rather than sitting as a distinct patch with visible lines.
Step 3: Add the warm-tan back and head

Apply warm-tan roving to the top of the head and across the back of the body in the same way: pull thin, place against the surface, and needle in from the edges inward. The warm tan covers the top of the head down to where the orange breast begins, and the full length of the back. Blend the edges into the grey-brown base.

Pull a tiny pinch of black roving, the size of a pea, and roll it between finger and thumb into a tight ball. Place it on the side of the head where the eye should be. Needle it in with the size 40 needle, stabbing at a slight angle toward the centre of the eye to compact it into a clear circle. A glass-head pin can mark the eye position before needling. Work both eyes to match in size and position before finishing.

Work the size 40 needle shallowly over the whole surface to smooth any surface fibre. The finished robin should be firm, compact, and hold its shape when squeezed gently. For a standing robin, press the base of the body flat on the foam mat and needle the flat base firmly to stabilise it. For a perching robin, twist two lengths of fine wire into legs and feet and press them into the base of the body from below, needling around the insertion point to lock them in.

attrs.intro: Three problems specific to this pattern.

items.fix: Work the perimeter of each colour patch with fine needle stabs at a slight inward angle before filling in the centre. This blends the transition from the patch into the base colour.
items.cause: The needle worked the centre of the patch first rather than the edges. The edges need to be locked into the base before the centre is filled.
items.symptom: Orange breast patch has a visible seam edge rather than a feathered blend

items.fix: Roll the tiny eye-pinch between finger and thumb until it is round and dense before placing it on the head. Use the fine needle at a slight inward angle, stabbing around the perimeter of the circle to define the edge.
items.cause: The roving was spread too thin or not rolled tightly enough before placement.
items.symptom: Eye looks like a smear rather than a distinct circle

items.fix: Drive the needle to its full length during each stab. Shallow needling only compacts the surface; the interior needs full-length stabs to build density.
items.cause: The roving was not folded densely enough at the start, or the needling was too shallow.
items.symptom: Body base is still soft after 20 minutes of needling

attrs.heading: Common problems
